comments.title: Updates to 'The Druidry Handbook' book recommendations?
comments.content:
JMG,

First things first: Happy 34th Anniversary to both you and Sara. And would you please pass on my thanks and appreciation of her comments about modern art in Ecosophia?

I've had my first read-through of the Handbook (once quickly, to get the lay of the land), and next I'll go slowly through it. Throughout the book, you make various recommendations for further reading, and I am wondering if you would still recommend the following books and/or editions, or if there are others you would now recommend in their stead:

1. Steve Blamires, "Celtic Tree Mysteries" (Llewellyn, 1997).
2. Geoffrey of Monmouth, "The History of the Kings of Britain" (Penguin, 1966).
3. Sir Thomas Malory, "Le Morte d'Arthur" (Random House, 1994).
4. Paul Rhys Mountfort, "Ogam: The Celtic Oracle of the Trees" (Destiny, 2002).

Finally, is there an introduction to sacred geometry that you could recommend?

P.S. I'm finding the orthography of the names in the book mind-bending. I'm going to have to do some research. That and I'm finding myself drawn to Merlin/Myrrdin (I think I must be the only person in the West who isn't very familiar with his mythos).

Thanks for you time and patience,
Mark Taber

replies.title: Re: Updates to 'The Druidry Handbook' book recommendations?
replies.content:
Thank you, and I'll certainly pass on your thanks to Sara.

The books aren't on the recommended list because I don't think you should read anything else. They're simply books (in the case of #1 and #4) or editions (in the case of #2 and #3) that I read and found suitable. They're still suitable; if you find something else that works better for you, why, that's good too. As for books on sacred geometry, Miranda Lundy's elegant little book Sacred Geometry is a good starting place, and Robert Lawlor's Sacred Geometry: Philosophy and Practice is a good next step.

If you're interested in Merlin, btw, I highly recommend Nikolai Tolstoy's book The Quest for Merlin as a first-rate introduction to the legend and its historical roots.

comments.title: psychedelics
comments.content:
I just finished the book How To Change Your Mind by Michael Pollan and I'm curious about psychedelics as a tool rather than recreation. He mentions finding a qualified guide for the journey (psychologist, shaman, etc) to get the most out of the experience.

Supposing a jurisdiction where legality is not an issue, is there significant danger if one is also regularly performing basic beginner ritual magic?

My guess is that it's not a good idea to do both at the same time, but I'm curious if there's a certain waiting period before and after like sex, etc.

replies.title: Re: psychedelics
replies.content:
I once had a long and very productive conversation with a person who used psychedelics of various kinds as a spiritual path. We were sitting in a forest in Oregon at the time, so it was a congenial subject! We talked over the requirements and results of our respective paths,and both came to the conclusion that while they're both valid, they don't mix; it's like trying to travel north and south at the same time.

Everything I've learned about psychedelics before and since then -- including my own limited dabbling during my first time at college -- leads me to the same conclusion. Choose one or the other, and whichever one you don't choose should be left strictly alone.

replies.title: Re: psychedelics
replies.content: Would you be willing to elaborate a bit on the conflicting requirements and results?

replies.title: Re: psychedelics
replies.content:
Sure. Magic is the art and science of causing change in consciousness in accordance with will. It works by developing conscious control over states of mind, and when you interact with what (for present purposes) we'll call the unconscious mind, you do it in a deliberate fashion, in which the conscious mind is never submerged. Clarity of mind, strength of will, self-knowledge, and self-mastery are essentials of the path of magic.

The psychedelic path, by contrast, requires you to be ready to give up control over your states of consciousness. While you're working with a psychedelic ally, the ally is in control, and it's only afterwards that you reintegrate your experiences with your conscious experience of the world. Clarity of mind and strength of will aren't useful in that work; it requires a receptive approach rather than an active one. (In this way it's rather similar to mysticism.)

replies.title: Re: psychedelics
replies.content: ... now I'm confused about what are the "magic" and "mysticism" you just contrasted. And I might have called you a mystic! May you explain?

replies.title: Re: psychedelics
replies.content: Magic and mysticism are the two primary spiritual paths in the Western tradition. Where magic is active, mysticism is receptive; where the mage is motivated by wisdom, the mystic is motivated by love. The mystic seeks an ever closer relationship with a god or some other sacred power, personal or impersonal. If you'd like to learn more, I highly recommend Evelyn Underhill's book Mysticism for a first-rate overview of the subject.

replies.title: Re: psychedelics
replies.content: In the (arguably Western) Muslim version, that's Sufism - but I never thought that was incompatible with "magic" as expounded upon by you. Will try to check Underhill's book - thanks.

replies.title: Re: psychedelics
replies.content:
Thanks for the answer. On a side note Pollan's book wrestled with the concept of a larger spiritual world despite his staunch materialism and atheism. Interestingly enough at the end of the book he did recount an experience of being inside a prison with a vine trying to show him the way out, but at the time of writing he couldn't (or wouldn't) figure out the meaning of those images.

At least occult studies have some road maps for what exists outside the meninges.

Speaking of which, I have never felt comfortable invoking by names of deities, neither for the cross, nor for the calling of the elements, and the animals leave me cold, so following your suggestions in the DMH, I adapted the invoking of the elements, and have been using the following for quite some time now:

replies.title: Re: psychedelics
replies.content:
Years back I used Magic Mushrooms for a while, and really appreciated some of the things the Ally I meet thereby had to teach me. An interesting story.

One winter me and my friends were living in a little suburban house, and the cold cold weather outside kept us in, staying warm in the basement. We couldn't spend much and had few recreations, and were kinda glum. But a friend from Denver had gifted the household a very substantial amount of magic mushrooms. So for lack of better diversions we were using them pretty often; which in this case means a little more than weekly. At first this was very profitable to me, as there were certain lessons and experiences I was given which were valuable to me, a couple were phenomenally beneficial to me. One day I was listening to music with the house mate I was least close to emotionally, and we took a large amount. It started out as such experiences do and then took a... not dark... took a serious mood.

In the dark basement with only the light from the fireplace and mournful irish ballads playing, I watched the dancing light on the objects in the room, and then felt next to me a presence, laying against the same rolled up elk hide I was resting my own head on against. The presence was like a old dusty cowboy, the type one could respect in that 'salt of the earth' sort of way. He be all like "We have taught you what you are ready to learn from us, how about you take a break from this?" the mushrooms that is. As the effects diminished I thought on that and figured it was true, I had crossed the point of diminishing returns on the relationship and it was becoming mere escape. So I stopped for some time. The following Fall a good good friend invited me to share a trip with him; and it was true, I had learned what was my share to learn from the Mushrooms. I haven't tried them since, its been a good while.

The Mushrooms had taught me many fantastically important things, and showed me depths to life that no amount of philosophy or study could have persuaded me of.; taught me to observe many subtle things in nature which before my mind had screened from my conscious attention. But, there was only so much to learn from them for my path, and having learned it, it was time to move on; for beyond this point they are but an escape. While I was learning they were fantastically interesting, once I had learned what I could they were kinda annoying and uncomfortable. I will forever respect and be grateful toward that spirit for what it showed me, and because they had the grace to tell me when I had had enough. So few drugs are polite enough to appear to you and carefully explain these things.

comments.title: Do I need to do something to protect myself?
comments.content:
JMG,

I wasn't going to ask this (one question a week is enough from me), but something unsettling and unusual happened to me this past week.

About 7AM, walking from the coffee shop 2 blocks to the office, as I do every work day, and as I have for many years, I crossed the street and coming towards me on the other side of the pedestrian walkway was a young lady, about 9 inches shorter than I, though wider. She looked straight into my eyes and gave me a smile. Suddenly, I felt the urge to move just a body's width further to the left, away, but not to cross the street or rush past her. Normal pace, steady. Her smile chilled me. I can't explain. And her eyes. She kept looking straight into my eyes.

Now this is going to sound crazy, but I knew she was thinking about attacking me. While she looked in my eyes, and while she smiled. Or maybe whatever was looking out through her eyes was thinking about having her attack me. And as we passed each other, a thought came into my head, clear as a bell, and said to me: "I know who you are and I know what you are."

Now, working downtown, I've dealt with drug addicts, mentally ill, homeless, you name it. This was the first time I got spooked. I haven't seen her before or since.

I've just started studying the Druid Handbook. Do I need to start doing a beginner's protection ritual now? If so, what would you recommend for a tyro?

Thanks again,
Mark

replies.title: Re: Do I need to do something to protect myself?
replies.content: You might find it useful to learn and practice the Druid's Egg exercise given in p. 233-234 of The Druidry Handbook. Later on, when you've gotten a little further into the other practices, you might consider picking up The Druid Magic Handbook and learning the Sphere of Protection ritual, which is a good general protective working.

replies.title: Re: Do I need to do something to protect myself?
replies.content: Thank you! I suspected it as much, but now I have confirmation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
"Let my people see the world as it really is" -- oh, man. I hope you wouldn't seriously consider a ritual with this intention. What you mean, of course, is "Let my people see the world the way I do" -- and if you think that your idiosyncratic personal viewpoint is "the world as it really is," um, I recommend a course in basic epistemology, and a good shot of intellectual modesty into the bargain.

For a good counterexample, look at the political magic recorded in Dion Fortune's book The Magical Battle of Britain. She wasn't trying to force anyone to change their behavior. Instead, she set up a network of people who used magical meditation to charge certain widely recognized symbols and ideas with energy, so that everyone in Britain who chose to turn to those symbols and ideas could draw strength from them.

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Mostly it's because in recent months I've seen way too many people insist with a straight face that the problem with Trump supporters or what have you is that they don't see the world as it really is -- meaning, of course, the way that the person speaking sees the world. There are two problems with that -- the first is that you don't have the right to force people to change how they see the world, the second is that you risk severe blowback if your own way of seeing the world is just as far out to lunch as the people whose thinking you want to change -- and what happens if the consequence of your working is that you discover that the people whose minds you want to change have a worldview more accurate than yours?

Again, Dion Fortune is a great counterexample. Her network wasn't trying to force anyone to take up the ideas on which they were meditating; instead, they were broadcasting a signal that people could choose to pick up, or not -- and having the Minister of War and the Bishop of York pick up the signal and give it a signal boost was a very good sign that their work was having its desired effect.

replies.title: Re: Do no harm, for fiction writers
replies.content:
Good heavens, no, you don't have to be concerned about that. People buy and read your stories because they want that experience of being swept away by the story; you have their consent -- they wouldn't start reading if they didn't want that, and they won't keep reading if they don't like the place you're taking them. (I'm sure you've had the experience of starting a novel, deciding that the place it takes you isn't somewhere you want to go, and stopping in mid-scene or even mid-sentence, never to finish; I certainly have.)

replies.title: Re: Access to magic
replies.content: That's one of those things that you have to grapple with for yourself. Magic isn't for everyone; it requires the same kind of dedication and focus that you'd have to put into becoming a classically trained pianist, say, or a martial artist in a traditional style. The great difference is that in magic, not only can you do it by yourself -- guided only by a textbook or a set of lessons -- but all the most important steps along the way must be done by yourself. The portal is in front of you; nothing requires you to enter it; and only you can know whether entering it is the right thing for you to do.

And I understand that the world's most accomplished remote reviewers can only achieve 80% accuracy or so at best.

Now of course different divinatory methodologies work for different people to wildly differing degrees, and a decent fraction simply don't have the divinatory knack full stop. But I'd like to have everyone learn and use the same methodology so we can workshop it and discuss results and implications in class. Or might the latter work more often, for more students, than the 20% hit rate they're currently getting with geomancy? comments.title: Multiple Pantheons
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I'd like to hear your opinion about interacting with gods from a mashup of different pantheons. For background, I am working through the Candidate year of the AODA program.

I am confused about this because on the one hand, you have made recurring advice here dissuading people from mixing different magical systems, and I can see the problems with confusing the symbolism of say, Lug and Tyr.

replies.title: Re: Multiple Pantheons
replies.content: Further into that: if calling on deities from more than 1 historical pantheon is feasible, are there risks related to calling on deities from different origins but similar in function/character? At the extreme: should one expect Thor and Indra to be the same deity?

replies.title: Re: Multiple Pantheons
replies.content:
Okay, to begin with it's important to remember that magic and religion are not the same thing. It was perfectly ordinary and very common for people in ancient times to worship deities from many different pantheons; for example, the Celtic horse goddess Epona became the divine patron of the Roman cavalry, and so cavalrymen who came from all over the empire -- and had their own traditional gods and goddesses -- also burnt incense, poured wine, and barbecued the occasional bullock in Epona's honor. So what you do in terms of worship is entirely up to you.

It's when you start doing magic that it becomes an issue. From a magical perspective -- there are other perspectives, of course -- a pantheon of gods is a balanced and complete alphabet of forces. So your magical workings need to use one such set of forces, rather than jumbling together a mess of different ones, for much the same reason that you speak one language at a time and write in one alphabet at a time. As a beginner, furthermore, learning the habits of one pantheon is quite enough to keep you busy!

Thor and Indra are emphatically not the same deity, by the way, and it's quite possible that they might get into the metaphysical equivalent of a bar fight, in which case you and everything around you could end up much the worse for wear.

replies.title: Some may call them weeds!
replies.content:
Hi Mark,

As a suggestion that works with the land down here, you need to invite more diversity of life forms - along with all the chaos that that brings - into your land. Yes, yes, people will tell you it’s wrong, but it works. I've got a fairly large orchard and it is grassy underneath the fruit trees. But if you were to stop and look closely at the grass you will notice that it is actually herbage and there are countless different species of plants and mosses growing even in only a very small area. And another area will have a completely different community of plants.

I'd have to suggest that if all you see is Bermuda grass, then you've (or the people before you) deliberately or unintentionally set up a growing environment that supports Bermuda grass. And that is the plant that you are farming, given the diversity of plant and soil life and available fertility.

I'll bet your farming mate enjoys the use of herbicides. If you sterilise a patch of soil and stop plants taking up minerals from the soil life, you might kill both and it looks nice , but then whatever plant is quickest to recover, well it recovers first - and in your case that may well be Bermuda grass. It puts you on a cycle, and who wants to go around and around in circles doing the same thing over and over again? Is that smart?

Gardens and nurseries are highly disturbed patches of land. Go and have a look at an undisturbed patch of land - and take a close look at some of the species growing there. There may be Bermuda grass, but observe closely how it relates to the other plants growing there. Using your eyes and brain - well that's magic!

And it isn’t just the plants that you want to increase in diversity, but amphibians, reptiles, insects, birds, and other animals too all play a role. Mate, the orchard has learned to cope with wombats and wallabies bouncing through it night after night like a pack of teenage vandals, but you know, those animals spread fertility. Your systems sounds constipated to me.

Chris

comments.title: Is Druidry Animism or Polytheism?
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
My question is about "spirits" versus "spirit". Reading your Kek Wars blog made me more aware that while I believe in a life force, and presence, AWEN if you will, I'm not sure I believe in Gods (plural) or other beings. The latter strikes me as a critical part of Druidry practice, especially what you teach. That is to say, I feel more animist than polytheist and you seem to be saying polytheism is crucial to Druidry. Have I got this right? Is it okay to continue without "feeling/knowing" the Gods?

For example, when I do the tree working, or call in the Names of Silw, Essus & Ellen, etc, I feel like I am evoking a quality, rather than an actual being. Perhaps I'd go as far to say that I call in a different energy for each direction. Is that enough? I don't get a sense of the Gods, whatever that means.

Many years ago I was an atheist, and this no longer works for me, so I am open to the idea that my personal cosmology may still be a long way from complete, but I don't know if I will ever know spirits, faeries or gods...

This question was evoked by two recent events. One was a person asking me if I was called to Druidry because I'd connected to nature spirits, or been called by them. Me: No, I just liked this guy's (You) thinking and got more into it the more I did.

His question made me wonder about my level of connection as the person is from a lineage of Welsh druids, so I felt a little remiss in that my beliefs don't yet have a strong sense of "Spirits".

The second event, counter to this, was I had a dream where a presence appeared in my bedroom and I was terrified by it. Not because it was evil or aggressive in anyway, just because it felt very powerful. I wasn't threatened at all, just awe-struck. It was like a beam of light energy, and although I saw no face or body, it appeared to occupy specific space and seemed to have antlers, very tall and vertical. I awoke with a strong feeling of having been in the presence of something extraordinary. What do you make of that?

Are dreams something that druids, or Gods for that matter, work with?

Thanks so much for you time.
Best,
Edward

replies.title: Re: Is Druidry Animism or Polytheism?
replies.content:
Ask three Druids, get five answers! I know polytheist Druids, monotheist Druids, pantheist Druids, panentheist Druids, animist Druids, and some whose beliefs don't fit any classification known to me. If you experience qualities or energies in relationship to the traditional divine names, that's what you experience; work with that, and see what happens as you proceed.

As for dreams, depends on the druid, and also on the god!

comments.title: Druid Wand Specification
comments.content:
Hi JMG.

Many congratulations on your anniversary, and very many thanks for taking the time out to chat with us on here.

My question is regarding the tools of magic, specifically, the Wand (its specification)

In the Celtic Golden Dawn Book, you wrote following in regards to the Druid Wand and its specification.


===========
"The Wand
This is for general use in magical working. The wand is a length of wood between one-half inch and one inch in diameter, and in length equal to the distance between the tip of your longest finger and the point of your elbow. (This is best measured when your arm is held up and bent at a right angle.)
An inch back from one end is a ring or band of gold or copper, and an inch back from the other end is a ring or band of silver or another white metal. The body of the wand may be painted or treated with a finish such as linseed oil or left plain, as you prefer.
The two ends of the wand correspond to the two currents of force discussed in the lectures of the Bardic Grade—the solar ray and the earth spirit. The gold or copper ring corresponds to the former, the ring of white metal to the latter. When working with the solar ray you will use the end with the ring of red metal (hereafter called the sun end) to draw in influences from above, and the end with the ring of white metal (hereafter called the earth end) to project or banish those influences; when working with the earth spirit in any of its variegated manifestations, you will use the earth end to summon and attract influences, and the sun end to project or banish them.

Thus, as will be demonstrated in the consecration ritual, the sun end is always used to trace the summoning pentagrams in the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram and the banishing pentagrams in the elemental and supreme pentagram rituals; the earth end, correspondingly, is always used to trace the banishing pentagrams in the Lesser Ritual and the summoning pentagrams in the others.
The wand is always held toward the middle, where the insulating properties of the wood will prevent an excessive flow of either influence from entering your body. For the same reason, either end of the wand may be directed toward your or another's body, but neither should ever be brought into direct contact with a living body, or closer than a few inches at most."

===========

However,

In the Druid Magic Handbook, you wrote the following about the Druid Wand

"Your first task, of course, is finding, buying, or making a wand that suits your needs and tastes. It should certainly be made of wood, should fit comfortably in your hand, and should be short enough that you can make ritual gestures and trace figures in the air in your usual working space without hitting the furniture or the ceiling. Other than those simple rules, though, the details are up to you."

Given the specifications above, why is the requirements / specification for the "Druid Wand" of the "Celtic Golden Dawn" significantly different from that of the "Druid Magic Wand"?. Is one necessarily and practically (magic wise) better and more effective than the other? if so, how and why. Is one more powerful at working with Solar and Telluric current than the other?

Please kindly share your thoughts and advice.

(Sometimes, us "newbies" tend to over-think these things or perhaps read too much into nothing)

Many thanks JMG.

replies.title: Re: Druid Wand Specification
replies.content: Let's see. Here you have the carburetor for a Volkswagen Beetle; here you have the carburetor for a Piper Cub airplane. Gosh -- they're different! Why, yes, they're different. In fact, if you got the carburetor from a boat motor, I bet it would be different, too -- and the same applies to the tools of magic. Each system has specific requirements which relate to that system, and only to that system; if you're going to learn a system of Druid magic, you need to use the magical tools relevant to that system, and follow the instructions in the textbook you're using to the letter. Otherwise you risk being like the person who wanted to bake a cake, didn't have two cups of sugar handy, and used two cups of salt instead...

So that got me wondering, is it something particular to Eurasian elms and not the elms of the Americas.  Apparently, non-American elms don’t have the slippery bark qualities for one thing. replies.title: Re: Tree energy
replies.content:
Thank you. That's a large and complex issue, about which very little work has been done so far, so to some extent you're going to have to experiment and see what works. One thing that's pretty clearly known is that differences between individual species can matter. For example, in some kinds of Druid magic, when the working calls for vervain you have to use V. officinalis, not the more readily availble V. hastata; their medicinal properties are identical but their magical properties aren't. So the fact that you're dealing with an American species rather than a European one could make a significant difference.

One point that comes immediately to mind is that European elms are infamous for suddenly dropping branches on people's heads -- as in, branches big and heavy enough to kill. It's something that European folklore discusses in some detail, and tends to be offered as evidence for the hostility of elms toward humans. I'm not familiar with the properties of U. rubra -- does it have this habit? If not, that might be a marker (along the lines of the doctrine of signatures) that U. rubra doesn't have the same hatred for humanity that other elm species do.

More generally, as far as I know there are no books that cover this issue. You may need to plan on writing one...

comments.content:

At the time, I took it as obvious that it would then carry on progressing through the veil to Tiphareth and then on all the way to Kether, eventually.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The analysis I used in Paths of Wisdom is more complex than that. We're not "at" Hod; what's happening is that in the present greater cycle of history, the second our species has been through, we're working the Path from Malkuth up to Hod. That Path is ruled by fire, which is the basis of our technology (everything from smelting metal to chemical reactions to nuclear power can be seen as an expression of fire), and its symbolism is that of a long harsh journey through a desert landscape -- you know, like the deserts we've created so many of on this planet during this cycle.

The previous greater cycle of history, the first after our species finished its descent into matter, worked the Path from Malkuth up to Yesod. That Path is ruled by Saturn, and that was the cycle in which agriculture was invented and the major technologies -- the standing stones, burial mounds, and the like -- all worked with the Saturnine energies of soil, seed, and death.

The next cycle, which is still some ways off, will work the Path from Yesod to Hod and complete the triangle. That Path is ruled by the Sun, and solar power of various kinds will be the key to the technology of that age. After that -- and we're talking many thousands of years for each of these cycles -- it will be time to begin working the Paths from Malkuth, Yesod, and Hod to Netzach: one Path per greater cycle, with many thousands of years spent on each one of them.

Our technology of fire is different from the technology of seed and standing stone that was in use in the first cycle, and the technology of the Cycle of the Sun will be different still. "Different" doesn't mean better or worse -- it's just a different way for our species to interact with the world around it. Our species will go through 22 such great cycles, according to the traditions I discussed in the book, before it completes its work and all the souls who need this particular biological form to pass through this particular stage of consciousness have done so.

replies.title: Re: Hall and Shingon
replies.content:
Yes, Hall was a 33rd degree Scottish Rite Mason as well as an initiate in many other Masonic degrees, and he seems to have been initiated into Jodo (Pure Land) Buddhism as well as Shingon -- certainly his book on mandalas gives a very thorough analysis of the Taima Mandara, the main Jodo mandala. My Japanese-American stepfamily practices Shingon Buddhism, yes.

Shingon is Mantrayana rather than Vajrayana -- it represents an older form of esoteric Buddhism. Two primary mandalas, the Kongokai (Vajra Assembly) and Taizokai (Womb Assembly) mandara, are the basis of the teaching; meditative methods start out with simple techniques (meditating on the seed-syllable AH, meditating on the moon disk) and go from there into practices of quite dizzying complexity and power.

I'm not a Shingon initiate, but I believe that Hall's meditative method in Self-Unfoldment Through Disciplines of Realization is based in part on Shingon techniques, though Hall also drew on Taoist and Zen practices. His stuff really is worth close study.

replies.title: Re: Gaelic Druid Revival
